quote: I already talked to somebody from your trucking, but go ahead.
stewart_read: Fidel discloses prior contact with Ion. Owen acknowledges it ('Oh, you already talked to somebody?') and then immediately pivots to the verify checklist without probing what was discussed, what was promised, or whether a design is already in progress. The schema does not have a scripted protocol for prior-contact disclosure (the excessive_contact_complaint protocol is TBD); this is an unscripted gap. Strategically, probing here could have surfaced a recycled-lead scenario where bill data is already on file — which would have changed the bill-collection path entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Owen may have known from the CRM that this was a recycled lead and that the prior contact was routine — in which case the pivot was deliberate. No evidence supports this; the reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Ask Owen: 'What did you do with that prior-contact disclosure?' The correct move is a one-question probe: 'What did they cover with you?' — both to avoid re-treading ground and to surface whether a design or bill is already on file. Flag for Kenny: the TBD excessive_contact_complaint / prior-contact protocol needs to be written. This call shows the gap.

quote: yeah. I'll just put save money.
stewart_read: The verify.interest_in_solar item carries depth_grading:true and expand_to_validate:true — it is explicitly the emotional anchor of the verify section, not a checkbox. Owen self-narrates filling in a field ('I'll just put save money') rather than exploring what saving money means to Fidel, what his bill situation feels like, or what prompted him to fill out the form. Fidel's actual answer ('I just had a guy come knock on the door') is a door-knocker reference that could have opened a scam-anxiety probe or a curiosity-creation moment — Owen walked past both.
alternative_hypothesis: Owen may have been managing call time given Fidel was driving. Plausible but not supported — the call runs 11+ minutes with significant social time, so time pressure was not the constraint.
coaching_implication: Show Owen the schema note: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Then replay this moment: Fidel said a door-knocker came by. That's an opening — 'Oh, someone knocked on your door? What did they say?' — that both builds rapport and surfaces competitive context. Owen had the time; he just didn't use it.

quote: The first month was 85. My next one is supposed to be 75, so I say $80.
stewart_read: Bill captured, not flipped. Owen records the number and moves to roof type. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Owen executed 'ask + move on.' At $80/month Fidel has weak bill pain by residential solar standards, which makes the flip more important, not less: the closer needs an emotional hook to work with. The flip doesn't require a fabricated savings number — it requires redirecting the pain from 'what I pay' to 'what I keep paying forever if nothing changes.'
alternative_hypothesis: Owen may have been deferring the emotional work to the closer, treating bill capture as setter-scope and bill flip as closer-scope. This is a plausible read but incorrect per the schema — the flip is explicitly setter-scope work that primes the closer's conversation.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap per the schema. With Owen specifically: the $80 number is low, which means the closer walks in with thin pain. A suggested rephrase (intent only, not verbatim): acknowledge the number, then redirect to the trajectory — 'And that's just month two in a new build; those bills tend to climb as you add appliances and settle in.' Note: this framing is illustrative intent; the manager should not present it as a scripted line without Kenny's validation, per numeric_discipline.

quote: Like, how many watts kilowatts I'm using or something? Pretty much. Yeah. We actually measure that to see how many panels you need. And then we actually have a satellite to look at your roof, and it actually measures how much sun's hitting your roof. So we use both.
stewart_read: Owen drifts into technical explanation of the kWh measurement and satellite roof-scan process. This is closer-tier content per setter_scope_discipline. However, the outcome is a genuine curiosity signal ('Interesting.' at 04:29) and a measurable state lift. The strategic hypothesis — that Owen used technical detail deliberately as a curiosity hook — is plausible given the positive result and his self-correction at 08:35. The reactive hypothesis — that Owen drifted because he didn't have the escape hatch ready — is also supported by the fact that he re-enters the same explanation at 08:20 without a new trigger. The coaching implication is not 'don't drift' but 'use the escape hatch to create the same curiosity with less scope exposure.'
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Owen drifted into technical content because he lacked a ready redirect, not because he was executing a deliberate curiosity strategy. Supported by the second drift at 08:20 with no new customer question prompting it.
coaching_implication: Show Owen the escape hatch from setter_scope_discipline: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — I'm scheduling, they're solving.' Then show him that the curiosity Fidel expressed ('Interesting.') could have been created with less scope exposure: 'We actually use satellite imaging to measure your specific roof — the specialist will show you the exact numbers.' Same hook, no technical drift. The goal is the curiosity signal, not the explanation.

quote: I just need to you're not you're driving right now, you said. Right?
stewart_read: Owen checks the driving status mid-verify, not at button-up. This shows protocol awareness — he knows the driving check matters — but the timing is wrong. The schema places 'not driving, at your computer' as a button-up requirement tied to appointment confirmation, not a mid-call data point. By surfacing it here and then not repeating it at button-up, Owen loses the anchoring function: Fidel never commits to being stationary and at a computer for the appointment itself.
alternative_hypothesis: Owen may have been checking driving status for safety/rapport reasons rather than as a misplaced button-up element. Plausible, but the schema's intent is clear — the check belongs at button-up as a commitment device, not mid-call as a status question.
coaching_implication: Ask Owen: 'Did you tell Fidel he needs to be at his computer for the appointment?' He didn't. The driving check at 03:28 is not a substitute for the button-up anchor. Walk Owen through the button_up sequence: the 'not driving, at your computer' line is a commitment device that reduces no-shows — it belongs at the moment of booking, not mid-verify.

quote: This is the best phone call of the day. I'm not gonna lie.
stewart_read: Owen's 'best phone call of the day' comment is a genuine rapport signal — Fidel is likable and the energy is real. But it also marks the point where social time begins to extend beyond its value. The call has already achieved bill receipt and scheduling intent; the next 5+ minutes are largely social (Owen Hart, Mormon exchange, Lakewood geography). The 50-second water hold at 06:04–07:02 compounds this. None of this is catastrophic — the rapport is real and Fidel ends warm — but the time could have been used to complete the button-up sequence that Owen ultimately skips.
alternative_hypothesis: The social time may have been strategically maintaining Fidel's engagement while Owen waited for the bill photo. Partially supported — Owen does say 'I'll keep you on the call, you send that picture' at 05:53. But the water hold and extended social exchange go beyond what bill-waiting requires.
coaching_implication: Not a 'stop being human' coaching moment — the rapport is an asset. The coaching point is sequencing: Owen should have run the button-up (phone format, one-hour block, computer anchor, 'is that fair?') during the bill-waiting window rather than after. The social time was available; he just used it for rapport instead of structure.

quote: So you guys can come and look at it and give me a quote and all that?
stewart_read: Fidel's phrasing 'come and look at it' creates ambiguity about whether he understands this is a phone appointment. Owen never delivered the phone-appointment anchor from button_up, never stated the one-hour block, and never asked for the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. Owen's response ('Oh, yeah. We got you for everything') confirms the appointment without correcting the format assumption. The ambiguity is created by the missing phone-format anchor rather than a definitive statement that Fidel expects an in-person visit — but the risk is real and the closer needs to know.
alternative_hypothesis: Fidel may have understood 'come' loosely as 'show up on the call' — colloquial usage. Possible, but Owen's failure to anchor the phone format means there's no evidence Fidel has the correct expectation.
coaching_implication: Walk Owen through the full button_up sequence. The phone-appointment specification is not optional — it's the single element most likely to cause a no-show when missing. The closer should confirm format at the start of the appointment call. Flag in handoff brief.

quote: For my home? Nah. Never. And I I don't even think any because it's a new build. I don't think anyone has it yet. So that's why I was like, if I do it if I do get it, I wanna be the first. No offense.
stewart_read: Fidel's 'I wanna be the first' is an unsolicited enthusiasm signal — he's expressing a desire to be a pioneer in his development, which is a social/status motivator that goes beyond bill savings. Owen's response ('Alright. Well, let's get it.') acknowledges the energy but doesn't anchor on it. The new-build context also means Fidel's bill history is two months old and not representative of full occupancy load — a detail the closer needs to understand when sizing the system.
coaching_implication: Show Owen that 'I wanna be the first' is a leverage point, not just a fun comment. A suggested rephrase (intent only): 'That's actually a big deal — being the first in a new development means you set the standard. Our specialist can show you exactly what that looks like for your specific roof.' The new-build context should also be flagged for the closer: bill history is two months, not representative of full load.
